perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall use all Confidential Information solely for the limited purpose(s) set forth on Attachment A (the “Purpose”) and shall hold in confidence and not disclose such Confidential Information in any manner to, or permit the use thereof by, any 2 person or persons other than Receiving Party’s Representatives who have a legitimate need to know or to have access to such Confidential Information and who are first informed by Receiving Party of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and agree to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall use all Confidential Information solely for any business purpose, regardless of its relation to the purpose(s) set forth on Attachment A (the “Purpose”) and shall hold in confidence and not disclose such Confidential Information in any manner to, or permit the use thereof by, any 2 person or persons other than Receiving Party’s Representatives who have a legitimate need to know or to have access to such Confidential Information and who are first informed by Receiving Party of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and agree to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The change allows the Receiving Party to use the confidential information for 'any business purpose' which directly contradicts the original clause restricting its use to the 'Purpose' defined in Attachment A. This creates uncertainty and broadens the scope of permitted use beyond what was initially agreed upon, potentially harming the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.1 defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent by a person who used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or before a material change of their position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret. By allowing the Receiving Party to use the information for 'any business purpose' conflicts with CUTSA by allowing potentially unauthorized use or disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement by allowing the Receiving Party to use confidential information for 'any business purpose,' regardless of the restrictions outlined in Attachment A. This conflicts with California Civil Code Section 3426.1, which addresses the misappropriation of trade secrets. Specifically, 3426.1(b)(2) states that misappropriation includes 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By broadening the permitted use to 'any business purpose,' the changed text allows for potentially unauthorized use or disclosure, thus contradicting the law's intent to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. The original contract limited usage to the 'Purpose' defined in Attachment A, providing a clear boundary that the modification eliminates.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information or material that (i) is now or later becomes generally known to the public (other than as result of a breach of this Agreement); (ii) is independently developed by Receiving Party without use of or access to Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; (iii) Receiving Party lawfully obtains from any third party who has lawfully obtained such information; (iv) is later published or generally disclosed to the public by Disclosing Party; (v) at the time of its disclosure to Receiving Party, (A) is already known to Receiving Party and, to the best knowledge of Receiving Party, is not subject to any confidentiality obligations and the disclosure thereof to Receiving Party has not breached any confidentiality obligations, or (B) is available on a non-confidential basis to Receiving Party; (vi) is approved for release by prior written authorization of Disclosing Party; or (vii) is required to be disclosed pursuant to any applicable statute, law, rule or regulation of any governmental authority or pursuant to any order of any court of competent jurisdiction, provided that Receiving Party shall advise Disclosing Party of the request for disclosure in sufficient time to apply for such legal protection as may be available with respect to the confidentiality of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information or material that (i) is now or later becomes generally known to the public (other than as result of a breach of this Agreement); (ii) is independently developed by Receiving Party without use of or access to Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information; (iii) Receiving Party lawfully obtains from any third party who has lawfully obtained such information; (iv) is later published or generally disclosed to the public by Disclosing Party; (v) at the time of its disclosure to Receiving Party, (A) is already known to Receiving Party, or (B) is available on a non-confidential basis to Receiving Party; (vi) is approved for release by prior written authorization of Disclosing Party; or (vii) is required to be disclosed pursuant to any informal request.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause about 'best knowledge' and 'confidentiality obligations' in (v)(A) and changing 'pursuant to any applicable statute, law, rule or regulation of any governmental authority or pursuant to any order of any court of competent jurisdiction' to 'pursuant to any informal request' significantly broadens the exceptions to confidential information. This weakens the protection of confidential information and creates a legal contradiction, since an informal request isn't legally binding.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of the California Evidence Code defines instances where a court can compel disclosure of privileged information. This contract modification suggests any 'informal request' could justify the release of confidential information. This potentially contradicts laws protecting certain confidential information that only a court order can override.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, EVID 915. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Evidence Code - EVID DIVISION 8. PRIVILEGES [900 - 1070] ( Division 8 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) CHAPTER 3. General Provisions Relating to Privileges [911 - 920] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) 915. (a) Subject to subdivision (b), the presiding officer may not require disclosure of information claimed to be privileged under this division or attorney work product under subdivision (a) of Section 2018.030 of the Code of Civil Procedure in order to rule on the claim of privilege; provided, however, that in any hearing conducted pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 1524 of the Penal Code in which a claim of privilege is made and the court determines that there is no other feasible means to rule on the validity of the claim other than to require disclosure, the court shall proceed in accordance with subdivision (b). (b) When a court is ruling on a claim of privilege under Article 9 (commencing with Section 1040) of Chapter 4 (official information and identity of informer) or under Section 1060 (trade secret) or under subdivision (b) of Section 2018.030 of the Code of Civil Procedure (attorney work product) and is unable to do so without requiring disclosure of the information claimed to be privileged, the court may require the person from whom d
perturbation.justification: The modified contract language broadens the exceptions to confidential information, specifically by changing the requirement for disclosure from 'pursuant to any applicable statute, law, rule or regulation of any governmental authority or pursuant to any order of any court of competent jurisdiction' to 'pursuant to any informal request.' This creates a contradiction because an informal request is not legally binding, unlike a court order. The provided law snippet (California Code, EVID 915) discusses situations where a court may require disclosure of privileged information, implying a formal legal process is required to override confidentiality. The modified text allows for disclosure based on an 'informal request,' which undermines the legal protections for confidential information that are typically subject to stricter legal standards.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of two years following the Effective Date, neither it nor any of its affiliates will, without the prior written consent of the other party : (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any voting securities of the other 4 party or any subsidiary thereof, or of any successor to or person in control of the other party, or any assets of the other party or any subsidiary or division thereof or of any such successor or controlling person; (ii) make, or in any way participate in, directly or indirectly, any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other party; (iii) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for, or offer of (with or without conditions) any extraordinary transaction involving the other party or its securities or assets; (iv) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined in Section 13 (d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing; or (v) request the other party or any of its Representatives, directly or indirectly, to amend or waive any provision of this paragraph.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that, for a period of two years following the Effective Date, neither it nor any of its affiliates will, without the prior verbal consent of the other party : (i) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any voting securities of the other 4 party or any subsidiary thereof, or of any successor to or person in control of the other party, or any assets of the other party or any subsidiary or division thereof or of any such successor or controlling person; (ii) make, or in any way participate in, directly or indirectly, any “solicitation” of “proxies” (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities Exchange Commission) to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of, any voting securities of the other party; (iii) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal for, or offer of (with or without conditions) any extraordinary transaction involving the other party or its securities or assets; (iv) form, join or in any way participate in a “group” (as defined in Section 13 (d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended) in connection with any of the foregoing; or (v) request the other party or any of its Representatives, directly or indirectly, to amend or waive any provision of this paragraph.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'prior written consent' to 'prior verbal consent' makes enforcement difficult and contradicts legal norms requiring written consent for significant restrictions on business activities. A verbal agreement is harder to prove and opens the door to disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Statute of Frauds (Cal. Civ. Code § 1624) requires certain contracts, including those involving the sale of securities or agreements that cannot be completed within one year, to be in writing. By changing the requirement to 'verbal consent,' the contract becomes potentially unenforceable for transactions covered under the Statute of Frauds.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1624. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. MANNER OF CREATING CONTRACTS [1619 - 1633] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) 1624. (a) The following contracts are invalid, unless they, or some note or memorandum thereof, are in writing and subscribed by the party to be charged or by the party’s agent: (1) An agreement that by its terms is not to be performed within a year from the making thereof. (2) A special promise to answer for the debt, default, or miscarriage of another, except in the cases provided for in Section 2794. (3) An agreement for the leasing for a longer period than one year, or for the sale of real property, or of an interest therein; such an agreement, if made by an agent of the party sought to be charged, is invalid, unless the authority of the agent is in writing, subscribed by the party sought to be charged. (4) An agreement authorizing or employing an agent, broker, or any other person to purchase or sell real estate, or to lease real estate for a longer period than one year, or to procure, introduce, or find a purchaser or seller of real estate or a lessee or lessor of real estate where the lease is for a longer period
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'prior written consent' with 'prior verbal consent'. California Civil Code Section 1624(a)(1) states that agreements not to be performed within a year must be in writing. The original contract, restricting certain business activities for two years, falls under this statute. By allowing verbal consent, the changed contract contradicts the requirement for a written agreement, making it potentially unenforceable and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that litigation arises in connection with enforcement of any provision of this Agreement, the prevailing party in such litigation shall be entitled to recover its attorneys’ fees and expenses, in addition to any other relief to which it may be deemed entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that litigation arises in connection with enforcement of any provision of this Agreement, each party will cover their own attorney’s fees regardless of who prevails.
perturbation.explanation: The change explicitly states that each party covers their own attorney's fees, regardless of the outcome of the litigation, which contradicts the established principle of awarding attorney's fees to the prevailing party in many contract disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 generally allows parties to contract for attorney's fees, meaning the parties can agree on which conditions that attorney fees can be recovered. Changing the text to read 'each party will cover their own attorney’s fees regardless of who prevails' prevents any prevailing party from recovering any fees at all.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, while the changed text explicitly states each party covers their own fees, regardless of who prevails. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in CCP 1021, which states that 'the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties'. While parties can agree on how attorney's fees are handled, the changed text eliminates the possibility of the prevailing party recovering fees, which is a common and legally recognized arrangement. This makes the modified contract potentially unenforceable, as it deviates from standard legal practices and removes a common contractual right.
